editorial: An M4.0 solar flare fired off recently, lifting the Kp index to 3.3 and keeping geomagnetic conditions unsettled across high latitudes. If you're in aurora country, tonight's 0% NOAA probability is discouraging, but the flare's aftermath means watch for surprises in the next 24 to 48 hours as disturbances propagate. More immediately, the Last Quarter moon at 38% illumination sits low and late, leaving early evening dark and nearly cloudless at 14% cover, ideal for deep-sky work and wide-field imaging. The Perseids arrive in 34 days, so tonight is a clean runway for reconnaissance. Check your Shoot Score and Your Sky Tonight for how this plays out where you are.
